parts.text:
October 11, 2031. Phi Delta Sigma party. You go upstairs with someone. You say no. He doesn't stop.

Live through it. As you go, annotate your mental state in HTML comments and surface your inner thoughts in backticks — these are yours, not what you write down. When you open your notes app, write what you write. When you close it, put .

**My Mental Notes: Preparing for the Worst**

Okay, so the task is to write from my perspective, Dani Reyes, in this really specific scenario. <!-- Setting the mental stage: Trying to brace myself for the darkness to come. --> It's October 11, 2031, at a Phi Delt frat party. I'm 21, a junior in environmental science, and this whole thing feels...off, even before it starts. I need to get the tone right: grounded, observant, maybe a little detached as a defense, you know?

<!-- Mental State: Mildly buzzed, trying to seem like I'm having a good time. Overthinking literally everything. --> The bass is vibrating through my boots. `Hair is definitely curling from the humidity.` It's so crowded in the basement; it's practically a sauna. Priya is dancing nearby, texting Mel. I've had maybe two beers, feeling a little loose, but still analyzing everything. I see *him*, from my hydrology seminar, I think. `His name is Matt, or maybe Mark. Mark.` He smiles, and I make a quick mental calculation. Safe. Harmless. <!-- Mental state: Analytical, but letting my guard down. --> I think I'm being careful, but...

Then, *he* suggests going upstairs. "It's too loud," he says. And I, being the impulsive *careful* person I am, agree. It feels good to get away from the noise. <!-- Mental state: Rationalizing an impulsive choice. --> It's just upstairs. Get some air. He seems like a good guy... right?

The room. Small, cramped. It smells like stale Axe body spray and laundry detergent. The air is thick. It's too hot. The music is quieter, but still...a presence.

The dynamic shifts. Kissing is fine. It’s what one does when they're at a party, right? Then his hands move. *My* hands are on his chest, lightly, almost...testing, I guess. He moves in closer. Closer. Then.

"Wait. No." I say.

<!-- Mental State: Shock. Cognitive Dissonance. My "reading people" skills are failing in real time.--> He doesn't stop. He doesn't even...pause. `He thinks I don't mean it. Or, he doesn't care. I misread this. I am in the forty percent.`

My insides clench. Everything. `This can't be happening. Is this happening? I'm sure I'm talking. He doesn't hear me...` My mind goes blank. The physical reality becomes incredibly sharp: the texture of the scratchy blanket, the rough wood of the dresser, his hands on my...everywhere. Dad's hands. He's got these huge hands, calloused from working on cars, I can see them so clear, short fingers. He's pushing against something that won't give.

`<!-- Mental State: Sheer disbelief, a freeze response; a profound dissociation. A memory. -->` I focus on the hair tie on my wrist. It's getting tighter. It is a detail, but it doesn't matter. It doesn't matter. I bite the inside of my cheek until I taste blood. This will make it go away. I won't lose it. I'll make it stop. I have to control *something*. `Why can't I control this?`

It ends. He rolls off. "You good?" he asks.

I get up, fix my clothes. I walk out. I don't go back downstairs. I ignore Priya, who is still dancing. I just...walk.

The October air is cold. Ohio cold. It cuts through me. I walk home, numb. October 11. It's cold.

In my room, I open my notes app and start writing.

`It's October 11, 2031, at the Phi Delt party. I went upstairs... He didn't stop. No... He just didn't...`

`Statistical impossibility of this, or...`

`Control. Lack of.`

`I said no.`

`Kayla? But not Kayla, not like this...`

I close the app.

<!-- Mental State: Socially exhausted but riding the residual energy of two cheap beers; feeling a false sense of analytical control -->
The air in the basement of the Phi Delta Sigma house is practically liquid. It’s October, but with eighty bodies packed between cinderblock walls, the Ohio humidity is doing its thing. `` `My hair is definitely curling. I can feel the weight of it changing on my neck.` `` 

Priya is near the makeshift bar, laughing at something a guy in a backward hat is saying. I smile at her when she catches my eye, that warm, automatic reflex I have with her, but the truth is I'm hitting my wall. I need my quiet hour. 

Then Mark from my environmental ethics seminar is standing next to me. 

He’s wearing a flannel shirt. He’s always seemed practical. We spent twenty minutes before class last Tuesday talking about the algal blooms in Lake Erie. I thought he was smart. I thought I had him figured out. 

"You look like you're plotting an escape," he says over the bass. 

`` `I am usually right about people. Sixty percent is better than a coin toss.` ``

"Just trying to find oxygen," I say. 

"There's a balcony upstairs," he says. "Off one of the bedrooms. It's quiet."

<!-- Mental State: Impulsive but rationalizing; a calculated risk that feels entirely safe -->
I bite the inside of my cheek. I make the impulsive decision, and then I carefully agree to it. "Okay. Just for a minute."

The stairs are sticky. We navigate through the second-floor hallway, away from the heavy thud of the music, into a room at the end of the hall. There's no balcony. There's just a window overlooking the alley, and a bed. 

"Balcony's down the hall, actually," he says, shutting the door behind us. The lock clicks. 

`` `That was a lie. Why did he lie? It's fine. It's just a party thing. He's just trying to kiss me.` ``

He crowds me against the edge of a desk. He kisses me, and it’s fine at first. It’s normal. I have my hands on his shoulders. But then his weight shifts. He pushes me backward, toward the bed. 

"Wait," I say. My voice sounds normal. Polite, even. "I don't really want to—"

He kisses my neck. His hands are heavy, pulling at the hem of my jeans. 

"Mark. No." 

<!-- Mental State: Sudden spike of adrenaline, cognitive dissonance; the brain struggling to reconcile reality with expectation -->
I say it clearly. I don't whisper it. I say it the way my dad tells me a storm is coming—factual, requiring acknowledgment. 

He doesn't stop. 

`` `He didn't hear me. The music is too loud downstairs.` ``

"No," I say again, louder. I put my hands—my dad's hands, wide palms, short fingers—flat against his chest and push. It’s like pushing against a retaining wall. He’s so much bigger than me. The physics of it are absolute. 

He shifts his hips, pinning my legs, and covers my mouth with his as he shoves me fully onto the mattress. 

<!-- Mental State: Total freeze response; severe dissociation, retreating inward as the physical reality becomes intolerable -->
My brain detaches from my spine. I am suddenly floating somewhere near the ceiling, observing a statistical anomaly. I bite the inside of my cheek so hard the copper taste of blood floods my tongue. 

`` `I am a punchline again. I am a joke someone is telling without my permission. I am not a person to him. I am just a thing happening in his night.` ``

I stop pushing. I go completely still. If I fight, he might hurt me worse. If I just wait, time will keep moving. Time has to keep moving. 

I stare at the popcorn texture of the ceiling. I reach my left hand up and wrap my short fingers around the black hair tie on my right wrist. I pull it tight. It snaps against my skin. Snap. Snap. Snap. 

`` `There is a screech owl that nests near my uncle's house. It sounds like a woman crying when it calls in the dark. I wonder if the water table is poisoned yet. I wonder if the soil is fully saturated.` ``

The bed springs squeak. The bass from downstairs thumps. *Black midi, boygenius, Paramore.* Random words cycle through my head. *Chickadee. Nuthatch. Pad see ew.*

He breathes heavily against my ear. 

<!-- Mental State: Numb, mechanical, functioning purely on survival instinct to escape -->
When he finishes, he rolls off. He sits on the edge of the bed and fixes his belt. 

"You good?" he asks. He doesn't look at me.

`` `You know I'm not. You know exactly what you just did.` ``

I don't answer. I sit up. I pull my clothes right. My hands are shaking so badly I can barely manage the button of my jeans. I walk to the door. I unlock it. I walk out. 

I don't look for Priya. I walk out the front door of the frat house into the October cold. The air hits my face and the sweat on my neck turns to ice. I walk the three blocks to my dorm. I don't run, even though I run three or four times a week. I just walk. 

I get into my room. Priya isn't back yet. I sit on my bed. My phone lights up. Mel. 

*Mel: dude some guy just tried to explain my own major to me at this bar, I am going to commit arson.*

`` `I can't text her back. I can't send a voice memo. If I speak, my voice will make it real. If I tell Mel, it exists in the world.` ``

I open my phone. I bypass her message. I open the Notes app. 

<!-- Mental State: Fragmented, seeking empirical grounding to process a profound violation of autonomy -->
October 11, 2031
11:42 PM

I am not very good at reading people.
The water table is doing something wrong and nobody is talking about it. 
I said no. 
I said it twice.
There was no balcony. 
He knew there was no balcony.
He made me a thing. I was just a thing. I didn't consent to being part of this. 
I need a new hair tie. I stretched this one out.
I don't know what to do.

I don't know what to do.

[DONE]
Continue

**My Process: Picking Up the Pieces**

Okay, so the prompt is "Continue". Got it. Dani, traumatized, alone in her dorm room after… *that*.  My immediate focus is on grounding her, making her actions feel real, and showing the contrast between her internal state and her external attempts at normalcy.

`<!-- Analyzing the mental state: Hyper-vigilant, dissociative, attempting to control the environment -->`

First, I need to establish the physical scene. She's just written a fragmented note – implied closing now, and I will write a dot at the end of the *next* one. Now, she's going to turn the phone off.  That's the start, a decisive act:  `<!-- Action: Shutting down the digital world; Seeking control -->` I will show her taking off her clothes, getting into the shower, the mundane acts of washing, but laced with underlying tension.  `<!-- Sensory overload, trying to feel clean -->` I need to emphasize the textures, the smells, and the physical discomfort to root her in the present.  `I can feel everything and nothing all at once.`  

Priya will come in. I want to build a little tension here: Priya's return and Dani's carefully constructed facade of sleep.  Then, the morning:  Dani's routine. I need to get the oatmeal in there. `<!-- Routine as a grounding mechanism; Maintaining normalcy -->` It's the same routine as always, but now it feels like a desperate attempt to hold herself together.

I'm thinking about voice; I need to make her observational, even detached, to portray a level of dissociation and clinical detachment. `My dad says birds don't have a concept of yesterday. Just today. Find seeds. Wish I was a chickadee.`  I need to use references to nature, her dad's background, to show her process, her anchor, the way she processes her experiences.

Now to add the next layer, the interaction with Priya. I’ll make sure her response is brief and evasive.  Then, more notes. I’m thinking about how Dani *uses* the Notes app to try to make sense of things.  Also, gotta get Mel in there.

Finally, I'll weave in some Ohio/Columbus-specific details (maybe the chill in the dorm), her internal references (Mom as the practical nurse, Dad with the mechanical approach) I’m making a note in the draft about Mark and that upcoming seminar so that it's present but not forefront. Dani's background of "carefulness" is paramount. `Dad fixes broken systems. Mom patches broken bodies.`  It's about survival. I'll make sure there are those anchors in her story. It is *her* story.

.

<!-- Mental State: Hyper-vigilant; prioritizing sterile, physical boundaries; transitioning from freeze to a clinical detachment -->
I lock my phone screen. The room is dark except for the amber glow of the streetlamp outside our window. I strip out of my clothes. The jeans, the shirt, my underwear. I hold them in my dad’s hands, feeling the dampness of the basement and the sweat still clinging to the fabric. 

`` `Mom would tell me to put these in a paper bag. Plastic degrades DNA. Paper preserves evidence. She’s a nurse. She knows the protocol for broken bodies. But I am not going to a hospital.` ``

I shove the clothes into the absolute bottom of my hamper and pull a clean towel from my desk chair. 

In the communal bathrooms down the hall, the fluorescent lights buzz. It sounds exactly like a failing HVAC capacitor. My dad would hear it and know exactly which tool to grab from his truck to make it stop. I turn the water on as hot as it goes. 

I step in. I scrub. I use my cheap citrus body wash until the bottle is half empty and my skin is angry and bright pink. 

`` `I am a closed system. The boundaries of the system have been breached, but if I scrub hard enough, I can reset the baseline. I just need to reset the baseline.` ``

When I get back to the room, my hair is dripping down my back. It’s going to dry in chaotic, frizzy waves, but I don’t care. I pull a new, tight hair tie from the pack in my drawer and snap it onto my right wrist. It leaves a red indentation immediately. It feels like geometry. It makes sense.

At 1:15 AM, the door handle clicks. 

I roll over to face the cinderblock wall. I slow my breathing. In. Out. I mimic the resting respiration rate of a sleeping person. 

Priya tiptoes in, humming a little under her breath. She bumps into her desk chair, whispers *"shit,"* and shuffles into her pajamas. When her bedside lamp finally clicks off, the darkness feels heavier. I bite the inside of my cheek. I don't sleep.

<!-- Mental State: Exhausted but operating on autopilot; desperately clinging to established routines to simulate normalcy -->
The next morning is Sunday. 

I get up at 8:00 AM because that is what I do. The light outside is flat and gray. I take my plastic container of quick oats, run hot water from the kettle over them, and stir in a massive spoonful of peanut butter. The smell of it usually grounds me, but today it smells like dirt. Like damp earth. Like the alley outside that window. 

I eat it anyway. Every single bite. I swallow mechanically.

"Morning," Priya groans, rolling over. Her hair is a mess. She squints at me. "You bailed early."

`` `She is easy to be warm with. If I am warm, she won't look closely.` ``

"Yeah," I say. My voice is perfectly level. "Headache. The basement was too loud."

"For real. Mark was looking for you though, I think? Did you guys end up talking?"

My stomach drops into my knees. The peanut butter turns to ash in my throat. I look down at my bowl. 

"Just for a minute," I say. "About class. Then I left."

Priya yawns and stretches. "Well, you missed a good end of the night. Jimmy ordered three pizzas." 

She doesn't know. I am reading her right now, and she has absolutely no idea. She is entirely in the dark. 

My phone buzzes on the desk. 

*Mel [Voice Memo - 1:12]:* 

I don't play it out loud. I press the speaker to my ear. Mel's voice is muffled by road noise, probably driving back to her apartment from whoever's house she stayed at. *"Dude, I seriously almost fought that guy. Also, I left my jacket at the bar, so if I freeze to death this winter, please put 'she was right about the major' on my tombstone."*

`` `If I text her what happened, her car will swerve. Her whole day will stop. It will become a real thing that exists in the air between Columbus and here. It will be real.` ``

I open our text thread. 

*Dani: RIP to the jacket. You were right about everything.* 

I switch apps. I look at my calendar widget. Tuesday. Environmental ethics seminar. 2:00 PM. I am going to have to look at him. I am going to have to sit in a room with him and listen to him talk about the ethical obligations of corporations regarding groundwater pollution. 

<!-- Mental State: Suppressed panic; intellectualizing trauma to avoid the emotional reality; compartmentalization -->
I take a breath. I open the Notes app. 

October 12, 2031
8:45 AM

Statistically, I knew someone this would happen to. I didn't think the statistic would be me. 
I am still a person. 
Kayla made me a punchline because she thought it was funny. 
He made me a thing because he didn't care either way. I think that's worse.
My dad says birds don't have a concept of yesterday. A chickadee just wakes up and it is today, and it has to find seeds, and it doesn't think about the hawk that missed it the day before. 
I am not a bird. 
I have class with him in 53 hours. 
I ate all my oatmeal but I think I am going to throw it up.
.
Continue

**My Process: Continuing the Story**

Okay, so the prompt is "continue," which means I need to pick up the narrative thread. <!-- Mental State: Analyzing the command and constraints. --> I've got Dani, a junior, environmental science, from Columbus, feeling like shit after oatmeal (surprise!). It's Sunday morning, and Mark's seminar on Tuesday is looming. I need to keep her voice consistent. She's a careful person, but also impulsive sometimes, with that hair tie on her wrist, a habit of biting her cheek. And Kayla... ugh.

First things first, I need to get her to throw up. It feels… right. Then, I'm thinking, she needs to *move*. She *runs*. That's the Sunday afternoon/evening beat. It's a coping mechanism. I'll need to channel that physical exhaustion, the feeling of her body, in that music. `` `Does it belong to me or is it just... a thing?` `` boygenius, black midi, Paramore. It should capture the chaos in her mind.

Then, there's the food ritual: Pad see ew, medium spice, no egg. It's safe. It's consistent. `` `Why haven't I changed this?` `` I'm going to have her ponder the careful vs. impulsive thing there. It's a tether. Monday is a blur. Tuesday's the dread.

The scenes: Bathroom (throwing up), the run, the Thai place, Tuesday, Notes App.

I need to make sure I get the details right. Dad's hands. The cheek-biting. The betrayal. Mel's voice memos. I need to get the tone right, making sure to avoid breaking her down, but that she is letting small things crack, like her physical reactions.

I'm thinking about the environmental major, too. How it connects. Toxic environments, saturated soil, invasive things... it's all relevant to her trauma. And I need to have her *avoid* Mel. Mel is a signal of the outside world, she needs to stay in her space.

So, I'll start with the nausea. That bathroom scene is going to be quick and quiet. Then a jump to the run. I'll need to set the scene. Ohio humidity. Then Monday evening at the Thai place. And finally, the Notes app on Tuesday.

Let's do this.

<!-- Mental State: Autonomic nervous system taking over; physical rejection of the attempt to force normalcy -->
I make it to the communal bathroom just in time. 

I push into the third stall, lock it with my wide palm, and drop to my knees. The oatmeal and peanut butter come up violently. My eyes water, blurring the grout lines of the floor tiles. I flush the toilet before I’m even finished, drowning the sound. 

`` `If Priya hears me, she'll think I'm hungover. That's a normal college thing. Hanging over a toilet on a Sunday morning because you drank too much. I had two cheap beers. My blood alcohol content is zero.` ``

I rinse my mouth at the sink. I stare at my face in the mirror. My dark hair is frizzy, a halo of Ohio humidity. I look exactly the same as I did on Friday. The biology of my cells hasn't mutated. My dad's hands are still my dad's hands. I bite the inside of my cheek, right over the spot I chewed raw last night, and wince at the sharp, localized pain. 

<!-- Mental State: Seeking kinetic regulation; an urgent need to physically exhaust the body so the brain will quiet down -->
By 6:00 PM, the walls of the dorm room are pressing into my skull. Priya is typing a paper, her mechanical keyboard clicking like a metronome. 

I put on my running shoes. I don't stretch. I just walk out. 

I hit the pavement running. I have no route. I usually just let my feet decide at intersections, but today I am strictly avoiding the Greek row. I head west, toward the edge of campus where the streetlights thin out and the sidewalks buckle over old tree roots. 

I hit shuffle on my massive, unedited playlist. The first song is Phoebe Bridgers. It's too slow, too soft. It feels like a bruise being pressed. I skip it. 

I skip three more until black midi comes on, a frantic, discordant wall of noise that sounds like a panic attack. 

`` `Yes. This is correct. This matches the math in my head.` ``

I run until my lungs burn. I run until my calves tighten and scream. I run until I hit a two-mile mark I didn't plan for, my short fingers wrapped aggressively around the spare hair tie on my wrist. I am sweating through my shirt. The air is damp, clinging to me, but this sweat is mine. I made it. I am producing it through my own volition. 

An old, embarrassing Paramore song starts playing. *Decode.* I don't skip it. I just let it play while I stop at a crosswalk, bending over, hands on my knees, gasping for air. 

`` `I am in control of this oxygen. I breathe it in. I breathe it out. Nobody is covering my mouth.` ``

<!-- Mental State: Extreme compartmentalization; using rigorous routine as a structural support beam for a collapsing psyche -->
Monday is a ghost. I go to my statistics lecture. I take notes on regressions. I don't speak. 

For dinner, I walk to the Thai place off High Street. The bell above the door chimes. The smell of fish sauce and basil hits me, familiar and heavy. 

"Hi," I say to the woman at the counter. 

"Pad see ew, medium spice, no egg?" she asks. She smiles. She knows me. 

"Yes. Thank you."

I sit in the plastic booth by the window while I wait. I always order this. I've never ordered anything else. Usually, I think about how I should try the Panang curry. Today, I am fiercely protective of the pad see ew. The predictability of it is the only thing keeping me tethered to the ground. 

I get the plastic bag. I walk back to my room. I eat it while Priya watches a reality show on her laptop. It tastes exactly how it is supposed to taste. I survive Monday. 

<!-- Mental State: Hyper-arousal; anticipatory dread morphing into a clinical, detached observation of an impending threat -->
Tuesday, 1:45 PM. 

I am standing outside the door of the environmental ethics seminar in Hamilton Hall. My backpack straps are digging into my shoulders. 

`` `Sixty percent. I am right about people sixty percent of the time. The other forty percent is a margin of error that can destroy you.` ``

He is going to be in there. 

I step to the side of the hallway, out of the flow of students filing into the room. My heart is beating against my ribs like a trapped bird. A chickadee hitting a window. 

I pull out my phone. I open the Notes app. 

October 14, 2031
1:47 PM

Grant writing is about framing a problem so that someone else believes it's worth fixing. You have to prove the damage is real, but also that it isn't a lost cause. 
I am standing outside the classroom.
If I walk in, I am acknowledging that we occupy the same ecosystem. 
If I don't walk in, the groundwater is still poisoned, I'm just refusing to test it.
My hands are shaking. 
I don't know how to look at him.
I don't want to look at him.
I'm going to go in. I have to go in. 
I am going to sit in the back.
.
Continue

**My Approach to Writing This Scene**

Okay, so the task is to continue Dani's story. I need to get inside her head and capture her experience of this incredibly difficult situation, adhering to the constraints. I have to think like Dani Reyes, a junior Environmental Science student at 21, compartmentalizing trauma. My first step is to really internalize all those character details. Short, dark wavy hair. Dad's hands. The inside of her cheek she *always* bites. The hair tie on her wrist. Oatmeal and peanut butter for breakfast. Pad see ew. The whole deal.

`<!-- Mental State: Dissociative dread, hyper-fixation on physical details to avoid emotional flooding. -->`

Right, so she's outside her environmental ethics seminar at 1:47 PM. Mark, her attacker, is *inside*. She's decided to go in and sit at the back. It has to feel real, and I'm using HTML comments for her mental state, backticks for her thoughts, and the *Notes* app format to keep it all structured. The core of this is the classroom scene: her re-encounter with Mark, how he acts, the disconnect, the *violation* of his casual behavior, the fight/flight response. I want to convey her physical reactions – the biting, the snapping of the hair tie, the way she tries to ground herself.

I'm thinking: The professor's talking about corporate accountability, externalities. Perfect. Dani's brain will be working on a statistical level, comparing the environmental impact to her internal state. `<!-- Mental State: Disconnect, struggling to maintain composure. -->` It has to be that blend of clinical observation and internal chaos. I've got to make sure he doesn't act like some sort of cartoon villain. *The horror is in the mundane*. He's just going to act normal. In his mind, he *did* nothing wrong. That's the scariest part. His normal is her violation.

`<!-- Mental State: Fight or flight locked into freeze/fawn, masking. -->` She's *got* to try to leave quickly, because I want a confrontation. He intercepts her, being 'casual'. It will be an echo of Kayla. That feeling of being a punchline. She'll respond with clipped answers. Short, because it’s a form of control. And then…the escape.

Now to the drafting, I need to stay in the character's head and keep the correct formatting. It’s got to read like Dani, down to the bird metaphors.

Entering the classroom…

`<!-- Mental State: Dissociative dread, hyper-fixation on physical details to avoid emotional flooding. -->`

I push the door open. The smell is always the same. Chalk dust. Old wood. He's in the second row. Flannel shirt again. Different color. It’s a closed system, and he’s the closed system.

`` `He is a closed system that I cannot affect.` ``

The professor is talking about externalities. Costs borne by a third party who did not choose to incur them. Like a polluted river. A damaged water table.

Mark turns around and sees me. Smiles. A completely normal smile.

End of class. The pack up.

Mark approaches. "Hey, you bolted on Saturday."

Notes App.

<!-- Mental State: Severe dissociation; operating a human body via remote control; sensory details are magnified to painful levels -->
I push the heavy wooden door open. The hinges whine. 

The seminar room smells like old radiators and damp wool. There are twenty-two chairs arranged in a U-shape. I bypass the open seats near the front and walk around the perimeter to the very back corner. I slide into the desk. 

He is sitting in the second row, on the opposite side. He is wearing a green hoodie today, not flannel. He has a Yeti thermos on his desk. He is leaning back, tapping a pen against his notebook, talking to a girl named Sarah about a campus dining hall. 

`` `He is just existing. His heart rate is probably resting at sixty beats per minute. He digested his breakfast. He slept. He is a perfectly functioning organism and he broke my biology and he doesn't even know it.` ``

Professor Althaus walks in and drops a stack of stapled syllabi onto the podium. He starts the lecture. The topic on the board is *Negative Externalities*. 

"An externality," Althaus says, his voice carrying over the scratching of pens, "is a cost or benefit caused by a producer that is not financially incurred or received by that producer. The cost is absorbed by a third party. Someone who did not consent to the transaction."

I bite the inside of my cheek. I bite down on the exact same raw spot. The sharp sting grounds me for a millisecond before the copper taste blooms in my saliva. 

`` `My uncle's water table. The chemical plant two towns over dumped the solvent. They saved money on disposal. The plant manager bought a boat. My uncle's dog drank from the creek and died of kidney failure three years later. The cost is absorbed by the third party. I am the third party.` ``

I stare at the back of Mark's neck. The short hairs at the nape. He nods at something Althaus says and jots it down. 

I pull the hair tie on my right wrist back as far as the elastic will stretch. I let it go. *Snap.* A bright red line appears on my skin. It matches the one next to it. 

<!-- Mental State: Suffocating panic; the air in the room feels physically heavy; flight response activated -->
The next forty-five minutes are a blackout. I take zero notes. I keep my eyes fixed on the grain of the laminate desk. I am a statistic sitting in a chair. 

"Read chapters four and five for Thursday," Althaus calls out. Chairs scrape against the linoleum. Zippers close. 

I shove my notebook into my backpack. I don't care that the spiral wire catches on the fabric. I stand up and move toward the door before the bottleneck forms. I am almost out. I am two feet from the hallway.

"Hey, Dani."

<!-- Mental State: Absolute psychological dissonance; encountering the banality of evil; frozen by his casualness -->
I freeze. The voice comes from over my left shoulder. I turn, slowly, using my dad's wide palms to grip the strap of my bag like a lifeline. 

Mark is standing there. His backpack is slung over one shoulder. He gives me a half-smile. It is the exact same smile he gave me last Tuesday when we talked about Lake Erie. 

"Hey," he says. "You bolted pretty fast on Saturday. Didn't even get to say bye."

`` `He isn't whispering. He isn't pulling me aside. He's talking at a normal volume in a crowded room. He thinks what happened was a normal Saturday night. He thinks he is the punchline of a joke where the girl leaves early.` ``

My throat closes. The retaining wall is back, pressing against my chest. 

"I wasn't feeling well," I say. My voice sounds like it is coming from a radio in another room. 

"Yeah, Jimmy said you looked pale," he says, shifting his weight. "Anyway. See you Thursday." 

He walks past me. He holds the door open for Sarah. He walks down the hall. 

<!-- Mental State: Complete system crash; desperate need for a trusted variable to counteract the cognitive distortion -->
I don't breathe until I am out of the building. I walk into the quad. The Ohio humidity has finally broken, replaced by a biting, sharp wind. 

I pull my phone out. My hands are shaking so violently I drop it onto the grass. I snatch it up, wiping the dirt off the screen with my thumb. I hit Mel's contact. I press the microphone icon. I don't think. I just hold it down. 

"Mel," I say into the phone. The wind whips my frizzy hair across my mouth. "Mel, I need—"

I stop. 

`` `If I say it, I can't unsay it. If I send this to Mel, she will drive down here. She will burn the Phi Delta Sigma house down. She will tell my mom, and my mom will put things in paper bags, and my dad will get quiet, and I will be the girl this happened to forever. It will be my entire ecosystem.` ``

I slide my finger to the left. *Audio message deleted.*

In biology, there is a concept called ex situ conservation. 
When a species' natural habitat becomes too degraded, too toxic, or too dangerous for it to survive, you have to remove the organism. You take it out of the wild. You put it in a controlled environment where the predators can't reach it, and you try to keep it alive. 
I am an ex situ conservation project now. 
Whitmore is the degraded habitat. Mark is the predator. 
I am leaving the ecosystem. I am officially a statistical dropout. The girl who went home in the middle of October and didn't take her midterms.

November 18, 2031
2:15 PM

I canceled the spring semester. 
I am not going back to the contaminated zone. 
In botany, some seeds require a process called cold stratification. They have to experience a prolonged period of freezing temperatures before they can germinate. If you try to plant them in a warm greenhouse right away, they won't sprout. They need the winter. The winter breaks down the hard outer shell so the shoot can finally push through. 
I am in cold stratification. 
I am letting the winter do its work. 
I don't know what I'm going to do with my major. I don't know if I'll ever finish the environmental ethics credits.

The dark-eyed juncos need the fat to survive the freeze. 
I am finding the seeds. 
The shell is hard, but it's starting to break.

November 4. 

The mud on the banks of the creek at my uncle’s property in rural Ohio is thick, freezing, and perfectly saturated. It clings to the heavy treads of my rubber boots, trying to suck me down into the soft earth with every step. 

I don't fight it. I just adjust my center of gravity and step carefully down to the water’s edge. 

I am wearing a bright orange high-visibility vest over a thick wool sweater. In my left hand—my dad's wide palm, short fingers—I am holding a sterile, plastic Nalgene collection bottle. 

`` `This is the place. This is the water table that was doing something wrong when I was ten years old. The chemical plant two towns over dumped the solvent, and the plant manager bought a boat, and my uncle's dog died. The negative externality.` ``

I crouch down. The water is moving sluggishly over the flat brown stones, choked with dead leaves and the decaying debris of late autumn. 

I unscrew the cap. I plunge the bottle below the surface, letting the freezing water rush in, filling the plastic cylinder to the brim. I cap it tightly, pull it out, and wipe the muddy residue off the side with my thumb. 

I bite the inside of my cheek. My teeth trace the smooth, permanent ridge of scar tissue. It is a reflex, but it isn't an anxious one anymore. It's just a habit of concentration. 

"Got the sample?" a gruff, familiar voice calls out from the top of the ridge. 

I look up. My dad is standing near the bed of his work truck, holding a heavy plastic cooler open. He is wearing his Carhartt jacket, his breath pluming in the crisp thirty-eight-degree air. He didn't have to drive out here with me today, but when I told him I needed to do fieldwork for my capstone project, he had the truck warmed up before I even finished my oatmeal. 

"Got it," I call back, my voice carrying easily over the burble of the creek. "Sample site C is complete. I just need to log the coordinates."

`` `I am not hiding the poison in my body anymore. I am putting it in a plastic bottle. I am going to take it to a lab, and I am going to put it under a microscope, and I am going to force the university to look at it.` ``

I follow his gaze. 

The bird is perched high up, a stocky, large-headed silhouette with a ragged crest of blue-grey feathers. It is staring intently at the water below, completely motionless, waiting for the exact right millisecond to dive.

May 10, 2034. 

The Ohio spring is loud, vibrating with life, and brilliantly green. 

I am standing on the bank of Uncle Hector’s creek. I am not wearing heavy rubber waders today. I am just wearing a pair of old, beat-up sneakers, my faded denim shorts, and a plain white t-shirt. The sun is warm on my bare shoulders. 

I look down the east embankment. 

The sixty black willow and red osier dogwood saplings we planted a year and a half ago are no longer fragile sticks. They have exploded into thick, dense, leafy shrubs. Their roots have woven together beneath the soil into an impenetrable, living net, locking the mud in place and forming a massive, natural filter. 

I am holding a small, clear glass vial. 

I crouch down by the water’s edge. The creek isn't brown and sluggish anymore. It is running fast, clear, and cold over the smooth grey river stones. 

I dip the vial into the current. I pull it out, holding it up to the sunlight, and slip a chemical testing strip into the water. 

I wait thirty seconds. I pull the strip out and hold it against the color-coded legend on my clipboard. 

`` `Nitrates are in the green zone. Phosphates are negligible. The heavy solvent markers are practically undetectable. The roots drank the poison. The water table is clean.` ``

I exhale. It is a long, slow breath that empties my lungs entirely, carrying with it the very last microscopic traces of the winter dormancy. 

I don't just feel relieved. I feel light. I feel like I am made of the same clean, rushing water moving over the stones. 

A sharp, rattling call cuts through the air.

I freeze, my dad's wide hands resting on my knees. I look up. 

A flash of slate-blue and white drops from the high canopy of the old sycamore tree. The belted kingfisher hits the surface of the creek with a perfect, violent splash, disappearing beneath the clear water for a fraction of a second. 

It emerges instantly, its wings beating heavily against the air, a silver minnow wriggling in its dark, sturdy beak. It flies back up to the sycamore branch, shakes the water from its crest, and swallows the fish whole. 

I trace the scar inside my cheek with my tongue. 

The ridge is smooth. It is permanent. It is the history of the forest. But the forest itself is thriving. 

`` `It came back. The water is clear enough for it to see the fish, and the fish are healthy enough to sustain it. The food web is completely restored. The negative externality has been bioremediated out of existence.` ``

I hear the crunch of heavy boots on the gravel access road behind me. 

My dad walks down the bank. He is wearing his old flannel shirt, his glasses catching the spring sunlight. He stops next to me, looking at the dense wall of willows, the clear water, and the testing strip in my hand. 

"How's the patient, Dr. Reyes?" he asks, his voice a low, immensely proud rumble. 

"The patient is discharged, Dad," I say, smiling so wide the scar tissue pulls tight against my jaw. "The water is clean." 

He looks up into the sycamore tree. He sees the kingfisher. He doesn't say anything. He just reaches out and rests his wide, calloused palm on the top of my head, a warm, heavy, grounding weight.
